data.title: Micron: TurboQuant And Capex Lag Is Not A Good Combination
data.description:
Micron (MU) is down 17% in one month, which is a clear sign that something big happened. This came after it beat EPS estimates by 25% ($12.2 vs. $9) and revenue estimates by 17% ($23.86B vs. $19.76B). Not only that, but gross margin doubled and operating and net margins tripled in 9 months. I can go on about the strong earnings Micron reported, but the market reacted by declining 30% since its earnings. With this kind of reaction, it confirms the thesis of my previous article, where I said what the market was pricing in made no sense. In its earnings call, Micron’s CEO, Sanjay Mehrotra, guided CapEx to increase by $5B to $25B due to expansion work at its site in Tongluo, Taiwan. Micron’s $100B megafab construction project is experiencing labor shortages and longer construction cycles, and its Fab 1 was delayed 2 years, with a completion date of 2030.

CapEx Lag

It is a known problem for Micron and other memory producers that it is impossible to time CapEx to meet demand for their chips. It is a highly cyclical business due to technological innovations creating a shortage and prompting Micron and its peers to invest in new fabs. As you can see in the previous table, fabs take a long time to build, and when these projects are finished, supply catches up and chip prices fall. I think it is relevant to show a graph I included in my previous article, as it shows how reminiscent this period is of previous cycles.

Because this time the high-margin, non-commoditized HBM chip is in high demand, it offers a cushion to its bottom line. This is different from previous cycles, as they involved commoditized products like SSDs and NAND. Still, with its fabs under construction for such a long time and the AI world moving so fast, demand for memory chips will be very different once it is complete.

Competition has intensified with the HBM4 chip; previously, with HBM3E, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) did not pass Nvidia’s qualification tests. SK hynix (HXSC.F) is still Nvidia’s leading partner, and Samsung has now finally passed its qualification tests. According to one source, Samsung and SK hynix have been chosen as the sole suppliers of the HBM4 for Nvidia’s next flagship accelerator, Vera Rubin. Micron later stated that it “has begun volume shipment of its HBM4 36GB 12H” for its Vera Rubin. Nvidia does not disclose its suppliers, but it's more than likely that all three have passed Nvidia's tests. In addition to Micron having fabs completed by 2027, SK hynix will open the first fab of the project three months early in February of 2027. This fab is part of the company’s $400B investment in a semiconductor cluster of four fabs. With all three companies competing to mass-produce the HBM4 and HBM4E chips, I really don’t think the shortage will last more than 2 more years.

TurboQuant

Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently released a compression algorithm for AI called TurboQuant that reduces memory requirements by 6x and achieves up to an 8x speedup with no accuracy loss. These tests were made on Nvidia’s H100 GPUs, and the results will change the industry. When processing a prompt, LLMs store intermediate information called KV cache so they do not recompute everything from scratch. TurboQuant compresses that KV-cache data, reducing the memory footprint by at least 6x while also speeding up a key internal step by 8x. Some estimates cite that “integrating TurboQuant into production inference servers can reduce the number of GPUs required to serve long-context applications, potentially slashing cloud compute costs by 50% or more.” There are debates among analysts about what this means to Micron and its peers and if it will affect the demand for the HBM chip.

I believe this is worrying for Micron as the memory chips become less of a bottleneck for AI inference and cloud computing. The reason the HBM chip was so in demand in the first place was that memory fell behind the speed GPUs operated at. At an 8x speedup and 50% cost reduction, data centers can use more with less, reducing their demand. It is also common to reference Jevons paradox, as it says that when something becomes more efficient to use, people may end up using more of it overall. This idea originated from coal: as steam engines became more efficient, steam power became cheaper, and more applications appeared. I would argue that Jevons paradox does not fully apply to Micron and its peers, although it will still affect the business. Jevons paradox is typically applied to resources that are not supply-constrained, which is the opposite of HBM. The HBM3E and HBM4 are already sold out, and Micron and its peers are not capable of producing more. Because of all of the other bottlenecks in the supply chain, Jevon’s paradox can only apply in the long term. In the short-term, HBM chip demand will significantly decrease. The worrying part about all of this is that this shortage is also short-term, with supply catching up in no more than 2 years, just like it has done in the past. Jevon's paradox may cause the demand of HBM chips to increase in the long term, but it will make less of a difference to its prices as supply catches up.

This means that, in my opinion, at some point its profitability will decrease and revenue will decline from its peak. I don't think the market is pricing this in, as in my last article I showed an intrinsic price of half of the market price at that time. I think this new algorithm also shows a fundamental flaw to Bulls: just like hardware is improving, software is as well. DeepSeek proved that more intelligent LLMs can be created with fewer GPUs, and TurboQuant is another of many more DeepSeek moments. Bottlenecks are solved not only by brute force but also by efficiency, and I don’t think the market is pricing that in as well.

Risks to my bearish thesis

The main uncertainty with my thesis is that nobody really knows how long this shortage will continue. With CapEx continuing to beat expectations and more uses for AIs being found, it is completely possible that this shortage will continue for longer than I expect. Also, because the HBM is not commoditized, Micron is not exposed to as much risk as before with its other memory products that Micron exited from. It is also entirely possible that the market is considering the cyclicality of its business with a P/E of 16x and a forward P/E of 6x.

The next generations of HBM chips, being HBM4 and HBM4E, act as a reset in competition. Just like Samsung was not able to pass Nvidia's qualification tests for the HBM3E, the same can happen as these chips become increasingly complex. Micron can gain market share from these situations and will create upward pressure on the chip’s prices.

Conclusion

I am a firm believer that cyclicality in memory chips will continue, and, with Micron and its peers ramping up production, in two years supply will be very different. TurboQuant offers an immediate solution to the memory bottleneck that made the HBM what it is today. While Jevon’s paradox proves that in the long term, the HBM will be even more in demand than before, this is not the case in the short term due to the fact that the HBM3E and HBM4 are already sold out for 2026. Micron’s margins expanded significantly due to HBM pricing, and with costs reduced by 50% and efficiency increased 6x, it is likely that its profitability metrics will decline. By combining both the short-term relief caused by TurboQuant and supply catching up, I expect margins and revenue to decrease in a few years. Efficiency improvements will continue to disrupt this industry, and chip prices will be most affected. Although there is no sign that CapEx will stop growing and Micron’s shift toward selling HBM instead of traditional memory products puts it in a better position, I still believe it is exposed to similar risks as before. I rate it a sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celestica: Why I Think It's Finally Time To Load Up (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Celestica stock (CLS) surprised me recently. I thought it will break down further. Yet, that didn't quite pan out. There was a little scare, but the buyers held the line stoutly.

I guess you probably guessed why. In mid-March, we found out that AMD has inked a partnership with Celestica “to bring the new Helios rack-scale AI platform to market."

If you looked at the print, it really helped to fend off further selling.

After all, the long consolidation from late 2025 has also caused some consternation as the stock dropped into a bear market.

AMD has been busy making deals lately. Under CEO Lisa Su's leadership, it is showing ambitions to take on Nvidia (NVDA).

Easier said than done, I know. so AMD needs more friends, don't you think?

And thus, the CLS deal comes in handy. And that also offers Celestica more opportunities to grow into the expanded CapEx outlook for this year.

Also, I think things are going to become even more exciting, with AI agents proliferating.

I admit that I kept a cautious rating on CLS in my last write-up. I didn't think that the AI buildout momentum could be maintained.

And, Celestica doesn't quite have the strongest margins in the tech sector, right?

However, the AMD announcement has kind of shifted the narrative for me. It shows that Celestica has more opportunities beyond the primary Broadcom partnership than I thought.

And it's not as if Broadcom is going into a decline.

And then, if we bring in the potential explosion of AI agents from this year as Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Code has shown us so far, it's hard to argue against CLS bulls, I think.

More compute, not less, right? These compute hungry and always on AI agents will gobble up the capacity constrained setup. For CLS, networking growth is going to be the next lever that will shoot through the roof.

In other words, a much fretted about slowdown may not yet show itself yet.

Maybe next time, but not yet. Not now, at least.

And we already know from Nvidia’s GTC that AI inference has arguably become the most pivotal opportunity from 2026, as AI agents look to scale.

Hence, it’s not just GPUs or proprietary systems that may dominate from here.

Now, we need a systems-level approach (across the leading service providers) to provide the optimal competitive edge for the leading AI chip designers.

Therefore, I thought the AMD tie-up really serves to bolster the competitive advantages of CLS as an growing ODM platform, corroborating its capabilities beyond just being a contract manufacturer.

This was also noted in the press release. It succinctly indicates that “Celestica will undertake the research and development, or R&D, design, and manufacturing of scale-up networking switches in the AMD Helios rack-scale AI architecture.”

We already know AMD’s Helios rack scale systems are poised for growth in the next few years, and expected to start ramping up from the second half of this year.

Hence, I thought it really afforded the necessary fuel to Celestica’s ambitions, which I believe should be accretive to its outlook in the medium term.

While I don’t expect these changes to affect CLS’s 2026 guidance that much, the recent optimism in CLS shares indicates that the market is expecting good news from 2027 onwards.

I thought that’s a fair assessment, as Celestica is already expanding its CapEx footprint for 2026.

Which we know was raised to 6% of revenue for this FY, as compared to the previous outlook of between 2% and 2.5%.

Still, for a company whose free cash flow margins are expected to drop to <2% for FY2027, I believe the market needs more convincing to assess that it could broaden its scope beyond the primary Broadcom partnership.

Nevertheless, I think being indexed to Broadcom’s growth shouldn’t be construed negatively, right?

After all, it's evident that the market is getting increasingly confident that its XPUs will play a more crucial role, as Google’s TPUs (GOOGL) (GOOG) gain clout.

And in Celestica’s case, the bullish narrative should look even more robust.

And AMD is looking for the right networking partner to spruce up its competitive edge, as it looks to gain ground against Broadcom and Nvidia.

So, I must admit that it has also caused me to reconsider whether this should open up more opportunities for upside potential for CLS in the next few years.

Here’s where the quandary is. Celestica is still expected to deliver >40% adjusted EPS growth in the next couple of years.

And I think that’s crucial to help justify what is, after all, still a premium earnings multiple that surpasses 32x (on forward earnings).

Moreover, when compared to peers, while CLS’s operating margins are well above the 5-year average (of <5%), I think concerns about whether Celestica could be overearning due to the current AI investment buildout aren’t immaterial.

Which is why I think you would agree with me that the market concurred that the AMD deal was inked at the right moment.

Especially since the stock had already lost some momentum from its November peak. Note that CLS declined almost 30% before it found a bottom post-AMD announcement.

Fortuitously for CLS bulls, the print has looked better ever since, suggesting the confidence that the market has priced in.

However, margins are still expected to stay under pressure, even as Wall Street upgraded Celestica’s estimates.

In the highly competitive EMS space that Celestica operates in, I think it’s always going to be tough as AI chip designers amass tremendous value in the chain and leave smaller setups like CLS to feed off the scraps.

So, forward CapEx guidance will most certainly be crucial, given how sensitive the free cash flow margins could be.

Nevertheless, I think as long as the market thinks that the AI opportunity could broaden for Celestica, as seen with the AMD tie-up, it should help assuage investors to consider staying on board.

Yet, I must still point out that CLS isn’t valued at a discount to peers.

In contrast, the steep premium over that of Jabil (JBL) and Sanmina (SANM) does offer us a timely reminder that a potentially steep compression phase could follow if execution were to weaken or falter.

After all, JBL’s 20x multiple and SANM’s 12.2x metric are well below CLS’s 33x forward multiple.

So, it’s really incumbent on CLS to help maintain the market’s confidence, although I acknowledge that the recent AMD deal provided the much-needed signal to shore up sentiments.

And I would point out that CLS’s bullish print continues to look pretty good, right?

Buyers flocked back to help the stock bottom out over the 50-week moving average (blue line).

That aligns with the previous time that it happened (back in April 2025). I annotated the chart with the green circles, showing us the strong conviction from that of the buyers.

So, while CLS did fall into an unfortunate bear market as the market worried about potentially lower FCF margins despite growing AI CapEx, the AMD deal was helpful to placate investors.

More importantly, dip buyers also appeared to have rushed in to defend the $240 level, which has held firmly for >4 weeks.

Therefore, even though I concur that CLS’s premium requires nearly immaculate execution (read little room for disappointment), the stock looks primed to follow through the next stage of its advance after the scare from November to March this year.

And that means that I think the risk/reward is more favorable now.

CLS is astutely spreading its partnerships (diversifying its revenue concentration), while strengthening its clout in the AI chip ecosystem against its smaller peers.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Teradyne's Easy Gains Behind Us - AI/Robotics Winner Expensive Here
data.description:
At a time of AI boom and the consequently insatiable AI chip demand, numerous stocks have emerged as beneficiaries, including those in the infrastructure, software, REITs, utility, and even construction sectors.

Teradyne, Inc. (TER) has been one such beneficiary, attributed to its unique automated test equipment offerings typically used in the semiconductor, automotive, mobile, robotics, and aerospace/defense end markets.

TER Proves Its AI/Robotics Beneficiary Status

These reasons may also be why TER has enjoyed an outsized stock price rally of +424% between the April 2025 bottom and the 52 week highs, with a similar development also observed in its semiconductor/robotic testing peers in varying degrees.

1. AI Beneficiary

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the increased manufacturing of semiconductor chips arising from the insatiable compute and data center capacity demand.

If anything, market analysts already project the global data center capacity to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," with it naturally triggering tailwinds for numerous data center-related stocks, TER included.

Here is where TER has benefited, attributed to its duopoly at 80% of the global testing market share along with a competitor, Advantest Corporation (ATEYY) (ADTTF) as of 2023, across the end-to-end semiconductor testing systems (compute, networking, and memory).

Given that 81.5% of TER's top lines (+7 points YoY) are tied to the semiconductor testing segment in FQ4'25, it goes without saying that the company has successfully capitalized on the "AI-related demand in compute, networking, and memory within our Semi Test business."

This robust growth profile does not happen by accident as well, given that TER calls the world's largest foundry by market share, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), a partner, one that has been in place since 2019, with it implying the former's multi-year growth prospects as the latter also scales their global foundry facilities across Taiwan, US, Japan, and the EU.

If anything, the AI chip market leader, Nvidia (NVDA), has already projected an outsized AI infrastructure spending of up to $4T by 2030, with TER's exposure to both the networking/compute chips and HBM/DRAM chips underscoring the latter's diversified AI monetization prospects.

2. Tailwinds Embedded In Performance Metrics

Given their well-diversified growth tailwinds from their prior focus shift from Automotive and Mobile end markets to Compute, it is unsurprising then that TER has been able to report excellent Compute Product revenue growth to $753M in FY2025 (+590% from FY2023 levels of $109M).

The same has been observed in their expanded DRAM opportunities to $350M in FY2025 (+337.5% from FY2023 levels of $80M), with these numbers naturally lending credence to the optimism baked into its recent stock price rally.

TER has further fed the exuberance through the outsized FQ1'26 revenue guidance of $1.2B at the midpoint (+74.9% YoY) and FQ1'26 adj EPS guidance at $2.07 (+176% YoY), with it notably accelerating from the FY2025 outperformance at +13.1% YoY and +22.9% YoY, respectively.

Readers may also want to note that these numbers are notably expanded from their historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +0.4%/-3% and a 10Y CAGR of +6.9%/+12%, respectively.

This is on top of the management's raised, "next few years" growth target of:

  • long-term revenue guidance of $6B at the midpoint (+88% from FY2025 levels) and,
  • long-term adj. EPS guidance at $10.25 (+158.8% from FY2025 levels),

with these notably expanded from the prior numbers of $5B and $8.25 offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, respectively, thanks to the expanded Automated Test Equipment [ATE] Total Addressable Market [TAM] from the prior estimates of $9B to $13B at the midpoint.

Most importantly, TER expects to achieve an expanded long-term market share of 46% (based on the management's ATE market size estimates of $13B and long-term revenue guidance of $6B) against the 30% reported in 2023, likely attributed to their growing partnership with strategic compute/robotics players.

Beyond the ongoing AI boom, readers may want to note TER's potential tailwinds in the physical AI, i.e., robotics, with the management expecting new product/service opportunities over the next few years, at a projected revenue growth from the $0.3B reported in FY2025 to the projected sum of $0.8B in FY2028, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +38%.

The same has been hinted for FY2026, with the ongoing ramp-up of its large e-commerce client expected to "triple-ish between 2025 and 2026 and then grow substantially post-'26 as the deployments go to a larger number of facilities," with it contributing to the Robotics segment's breakeven guidance in FY2026 and positive margin guidance afterwards.

These reasons may also be why the consensus has already buoyed TER's stock prices to new highs while raising their forward estimates, with the testing company expected to report excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +22.6%/+38.3% through FY2028, respectively - somewhat in line with the management's raised long-term targets.

3. Premium Baked Into Valuations

As observed in the outsized +370% rally from the 52-week lows, it is apparent that the market is rather exuberant about TER's testing prospects, based on the premium already baked into its FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 49.44x, compared to the 5Y mean of 32.58x and the sector median of 21.52x.

Thanks to the consistently raised consensus adj. EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +38.3%, TER continues to appear compelling at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.29x, compared to the 5Y mean of 2.13x and the sector median of 1.32x.

If anything, readers may want to note that the market's exuberance surrounding TER has also been observed in its Testing Equipment peers in varying degrees, including:

  • Semiconductor Test - Advantest Corporation at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 54.78x against the 5Y mean of 38.27x,
  • Semiconductor Test - Cohu, Inc. (COHU) at 60.07x/45.32x,
  • traditional Robotics Test - ABB (ABBNY)(ABB:CA) at 28.50x/23.08x,
  • autonomous mobile robots - Omron Corporation (OMRNY) at 37.20x/30.30x,
  • autonomous mobile robots - Rockwell Automation (ROK) at 30.07x/27.91x,
  • product test - Keysight Technologies (KEYS) at 32.81x/23.33x,

with it apparent that the expanded Total Addressable Market [TAM] arising from the cloud super cycle (semiconductor testing) and robotic testing have contributed to the premium embedded in their current P/E valuations against historical levels.

With analysts projecting the Semiconductor Test Equipment Market size expansion from $15.06B in 2025 to $21.59B in 2031 at a CAGR of +6.1%, I can understand why TER has been well buoyed thus far, thanks to their diversified AI/robotics opportunities and their projected market share expansion.

TER Is At Best A Hold Here

For now, TER has been consolidating along the February/March 2026 support levels of $273s and resistance levels of $344s, with it apparent that the 2025 rally has occurred overly fast and furious.

This conjecture has been confirmed through TER's overbought technical indicators across the trading volumes as well, with the stock likely to consolidate along these levels in the near term.

This reason is also why TER is unlikely to sustain the premium P/E valuations at over 40x/50x, given the notable gap against the peer group's current P/E mean of 41.80x and the peer group's 5Y P/E mean of 31.50x.

The same has been observed in the ongoing sideways trading, with TER seemingly growing into its premium P/E valuations from the recent highs of 54.67x to the current levels of 49.44x and, perhaps, eventually moderating nearer to its 5Y P/E mean of 32.58x.

As a result of the baked-in upside potential to my long-term price target of $333.90, based on TER's 5Y P/E mean of 32.58x (nearer to the peer group 5Y P/E mean of 31.50x) and the management's long-term adj EPS guidance at $10.25, I prefer to cautiously initiate a Hold here.

Given that much of its easy gains are already behind us, 2026 may bring forth a year of mixed stock price performance, as similarly observed in numerous AI market leaders, such as NVDA since August 2025 and Broadcom (AVGO) since September 2025, after the AI exuberance notably cools.

This is especially since the market sentiments lean on the pessimistic side, attributed to the uncertain macroeconomic/geopolitical environment and the current market rotation to value/dividend/safe haven assets, with TER likely to offer an underwhelming return profile in the intermediate term.

data.title: Turning Point Brands Remains Strong Despite Pause To FDA PMTA Fast-Tracking
data.description:
Turning Point Brands, Inc. (TPB) saw its stock price suddenly drop 8% last week. It dropped from $78.54 to $72.16 last Wednesday after news broke that the FDA would no longer fast-track Premarket Tobacco Production Applications (PMTA). The company has two applications with the FDA for its white pouch nicotine products.

I last covered the company on Seeking Alpha back in August 2023. The stock price has gained 185% since then, but a lot has changed with the company. Since then, Turning Point Brands has brought two white pouch nicotine brands to market: FRE and ALP. The new segment called Modern Oral has become the focus of the company’s strategy.

The company reported strong Q4 financial performance last month with net income and free cash flow. The stock price is up 20% over the last year. The stock price and the company are overvalued based on key metrics, like book value per share and backward multipliers. The stock price fell last week due to panic selling. I rate the company as a Hold for now.

Operations

Turning Point Brands owns the iconic Zig-Zag rolling paper brand. The company sells cannabis support products like papers and cones. The company also sells chewing tobacco under the brand names Stoker’s and Beech-Nut. Both operating segments have fueled revenue growth for the company.

Recently, the company has turned to white pouch nicotine products under the brand names FRE and ALP. The new products have been very successful and have helped increase the company’s financial performance. The company is now focused on growing these two brands.

Panic Selling

News came out last week that the FDA would no longer fast-track PMTA applications. These applications are required for a company to sell and to advertise any tobacco or nicotine products. The FDA makes sure that the products and their advertising are suitable for public consumption and not targeted towards children. To date, the FDA has only approved the applications for 20 ZYN products.

A company is still able to sell their tobacco or nicotine products while the PMTA application awaits approval. The process takes many years to complete. While the application is being reviewed, the products are sold under the enforcement discretion of the FDA. The FDA had been fast tracking applications under a pilot program but it has now stopped.

Investors took the news poorly and sold stocks of companies with pending PMTA applications, like Turning Point Brands. The move was out of panic. The end of fast-tracking applications does not impede the company’s ability to sell its nicotine products. It may limit the company’s ability to launch new products, which may affect competition and the company’s attempt to keep up with market demand.

Turning Point Brands has applications in the process for its two white pouch nicotine products. The ruling will not affect the company’s sales nor the popularity of its new products. The panic selling does not reflect the company’s financial performance and positive future outlook.

Strong Q4 2025 Results

Turning Point Brands reported Q4 revenue of $121 million, an increase of 29.2% YoY and an increase of 1.7% QoQ. The Stroker segment brought in $81.0 million, an increase of 69.5% YoY. The increase was driven by Modern Oral sales ($41.3), which increased 266% YoY and made up 34% of total revenue. Zig-Zag revenue was $40.0 million, a decrease of 12.8% YoY. Zig-Zag made up 33% of total revenue. The decline in revenue from Zig-Zag products was expected by the company. Gross profit was $67.7 million, a 29% increase YoY and a decrease of 3.8% QoQ. Profit margin was 55.9% versus 56% YoY and 59.1% QoQ.

Net income was $8.2 million versus a net income of $9.7 million YoY and a net income of $21.1 million QoQ. Total operating expenses increased to $47.7 million. The increase was due to ramping up Modern Oral marketing and distribution. Free cash flow was $19.2 million. Cash and ST investments increased to $222.8 million. Total liquidity at the end of the quarter was $222.8 million. Net debt is currently $77.2 million.

Market consensus for the company’s Q1 2026 revenue is $122.47 million, an increase YoY and QoQ. The company will report in May. Turning Points Brands expects its white pouch nicotine products to be a major synergy over 2026.

Revenue has increased for the company due to the sales of its new Modern Oral products. The company expects the trend to continue through 2026. The company consistently reports net income and free cash flow. Cash and ST investments have increased. Total assets outweigh liabilities. Net debt and total debt are in proportion to quarterly revenue.

The company and its stock price are overvalued. The price trades far above its book value per share and NAV per share values. Market cap and total EV are about even. Backward multipliers, like Total EV / Revenue, imply overvaluation. Regardless, the stock price should remain on its long-term uptrend. Overvaluation is the main reason that I rate the company as a Hold.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Turning Point Brands is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company has increasing revenues and consistently reports free cash flow. Debt is in proportion to earnings and assets outweigh liabilities. The company has plenty of resources to continue expanding its Moral Oral products.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a low to moderate risk. There was panic selling from the news about the FDA. Any adverse ruling from the FDA could affect the company’s performance or make the stock price drop. The stock price is overvalued and it may take time for the company’s fundamental performance to equal the stock price. Greater market volatility, as well as sector volatility will continue to affect the stock price.

Conclusion

News concerning the curtailment of the FDA’s fast track pilot program for PMTA applications caused Turning Point Brands stock price to fall. Other companies with pending applications also felt the volatility. The drop in price amounted to panic selling, since the news hardly affects the financial performance of the companies involved and does not stop their ability to sell nicotine products. Turning Point Brands reported strong Q4 performance last month and shared positive forward outlook. The company’s new Modern Oral products have boosted revenue and performance. The stock price is currently overvalued. I rate the company as a Hold.

data.title: 20 BDCs And 13 Of Them Are Likely To Slash Their Dividends This Year
data.description:
Introduction

The most common reason why investors deploy capital in BDCs (BIZD) is to capture double-digit income. Price appreciation is secondary, which is only logical given that at the end of the day we are talking about fixed income products (i.e., private credit assets).

Every now and then BDCs become subject to heightened pessimism and get sent down deep into a discount zone. In such instances it might make sense to invest not only for income but also for short- to medium-term price appreciation that would stem from P/NAV recovery.

I would say that today, with the sector-wide discount of 28% (below NAV), we have entered such a phase.

Now, even though I am extremely bullish on BDCs, as I have laid it out in many of my previous articles (e.g., After Recognising This, I've Decided To Go All In On BDCs), my base case is that we will see many BDC dividend cuts this year.

For some BDC investors, this might sound illogical, especially knowing that additional interest rate cuts are very unlikely to happen this year. This way, BDCs should be better positioned to preserve their current NII per share generation levels as the total yields of their floating loan books remain unchanged (or even go up a bit given the rising spread environment).

However, such kind of thinking fails to account for several undercurrents that are not as visible or straightforward.

In the article below I will guide you through my logic for why I expect many BDCs to slash their dividends this year.

Methodology

Here I will try to spell out in as concise and understandable a manner as possible how I have approached the BDC dividend sustainability assessment.

First, I have scoped out all BDCs that have their base dividends covered at anything that is greater than 110% (i.e., the NII per share should exceed the base dividend by 10%+).

BDCs with strong base dividend coverage levels, because of the built-in cushion, should be well-positioned to, in the worst case, survive 2026 without revisiting their dividend lower.

Second, I have gathered PIK statistics for all of the remaining BDCs to adjust the base dividend coverage level for non-cash income streams ("Adj.coverage" column).

By excluding PIK income, we can better see how sustainable the actual cash base dividend coverage is. The bigger the gap from 100%, the more reliant the BDC is on leverage to keep the dividend funded.

Third, I have included debt-to-equity levels for all BDCs ("D/E" column).

Anything that comes very close to 1.25x or exceeds it is an indication of elevated financial risk and, importantly, that the BDC does not have much gas in the tank to use leverage for dividend coverage. The higher the debt-to-equity goes, the more serious BDC should be about reducing the dividend to deleverage the books.

Fourth, I have assessed all Q4 2025 earnings reports of the relevant BDCs to understand whether in 2026 there will be any unfavorable debt rollovers in place (unfavorable refinancing or "Unf.Refys" columns).

The answer is "Yes" if a BDC has an outstanding fixed-rate note that is below 5% with a maturity date in 2025. The answer is "Yes" for BDCs that have made unfavorable rollovers in late Q4 2025 because here the negative effects will pop up only in Q1 2026 figures (not Q4 2025, which are used in my assessments).

Fifth, I have included an equity focus column to highlight BDCs that carry material equity stakes in their portfolios.

The idea is that these equity stakes can be potentially monetized to bring in equity-like returns, which could support the deleveraging process or simply keep the dividend alive (if it is undercovered) for a longer period.

Sixth, I have inserted a premium column to show which BDCs trade above NAV.

BDCs that trade above NAV have the luxury to issue fresh equity in an accretive manner. This capital could be used for portfolio growth purposes that would result in incremental NII per share generation (and thus enhanced dividend coverage).

Finally, the column "Cut?" reflects my views on whether I think that a particular BDC will cut its dividend this year or not.

The logic behind the "No" (i.e., the right conditions for no cuts in 2026):

  • The adjusted base dividend coverage is ~100% and the debt-to-equity is materially below 1.25x.
  • The adj. coverage is below 100% but there is a decent leverage capacity in combination with equity co-investments in the book.
  • The P/NAV multiple is above 1.0x (there is a premium in place).

All other combinations should, in my humble opinion, lead to a dividend cut.

Results (13 of 20 are likely to cut)

In the table below, we can see how many BDCs that have their base dividend covered at 110% or below are well-positioned to keep producing stable current income streams this year. I have marked these BDCs in blue.

Now, I would like to quickly explain why I have marked Gladstone Investment (GAIN) and Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) in blue. Optically, their adjusted base dividend coverage levels look extremely unsustainable.

However, here is the thing.

When it comes to GAIN we have to appreciate that it has entered into a significant growth mode, expanding its portfolio at a rapid pace. There always is a lag between when the debt is assumed (reflected on the books) and when the fresh capital gets deployed and starts to generate accretive NII. This is what we are seeing now. Plus, GAIN has, arguably, one of the strongest equity positions in the game (43% of its book is linked to equity holdings). Mainly because of the high-quality equity book, GAIN in Q4 was able to record a NAV per share growth of 10% compared to the prior quarter. In other words, GAIN has the right tailwinds to its Q4 NII per share and an attractive equity book that should help preserve the dividend going forward.

Speaking of OTF, it boils down to the same essence as for GAIN. For example, in Q3 OTF had a debt-to-equity of ~0.60x; now it is close to 0.80x. This is a massive growth that is likely to continue until optimal capital structure is reached (at around 1.15x). On top of that, OTF has a meaningful equity book that, among other things, includes SpaceX, which could be successfully monetized. Plus, the management has sent a clear signal that it remains committed to the current base dividend.

Key takeaways

As you could see in the table above, most BDCs have very tight base dividend coverage levels, which get worse if we adjust them for non-cash income (PIK).

The situation gets even more challenging if we consider the balance sheet capacities, which for quite many BDCs are exhausted or, worse, in a territory that would require deleveraging efforts (obviously negative to NII per share).

Some BDCs have equity portfolios that might bring in sizeable gains that could be used for deleveraging or portfolio growth. However, I would not assign too high a probability of that happening this year because of the elevated uncertainty that we have across the board (bad for M&A).

Issuing fresh equity is a rare luxury in the BDC space now and is only available for those BDCs that have high-quality assets and a decent margin of safety (which already imply sustainable payouts).

To make the matter worse, I even would suggest taking the Q4 NII per share generation levels with a grain of salt (and thus the adjusted base dividend coverage levels that I've reflected above). Here are the main reasons:

  • Many BDCs have unfavorable (below market rate) debt refinancings on the horizon.
  • The realized base rate cuts that took place in Q4 have some lagged effects that in Q4 2025 earnings figures have not yet percolated through the system (i.e., there will be lower SOFR driven headwinds in Q1 2026).
  • And this is the most important aspect - the BDC non-accruals are currently very low, and it should not come as a surprise that we see an uptick here quite soon (think of SaaS risks, inflationary dynamics, uncertainty and consequences from the war in Iran etc.). Rising non-accruals can quickly destroy all margin of safety and lead to sizeable NAV (and dividend) reductions. For example, before Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL), TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) slashed their dividend due to surging non-accruals, their base dividend coverage levels stood (approximately) between 115% and 120%.

So, I would not be surprised seeing Ares Capital (ARCC) and SLR Investment (SLRC) (two BDCs that I don't expect cutting this year) revisit their dividend downwards if they saw a 1.5%+ increase in non-accruals. The margin of safety is not that strong for them.

Final comments

I would suggest investors ignore or at least deemphasize BDCs that I have not marked in blue if experiencing a dividend cut is a clear no-go.

However, I would also like to underscore that even after dividend cuts, most post-cut yields should remain at attractive levels, mainly because of the system-wide discounts.

The average base dividend cut that has been realized so far by quite many BDCs is around 25%. Let's say that Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) which is a high-quality BDC cuts its dividend by 20%, we would still be left with a nice 10.3% yielding income machine.

In a nutshell, I would not make my decisions on which BDCs to invest in or not just based on the near-term base dividend prospects. I would use this information as a solid starting point to contextualize the case with, say, P/NAV multiple and loan book quality.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Strong Franchise, Limited Upside At Current Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) has an established position as a quality defense franchise that will continue to be relevant into the future; however, I see limited short-term upside given today's valuation. The leadership position that Lockheed maintains in next-generation technologies, including hypersonics, will continue to create a barrier to entry and reinforce Lockheed's competitive advantage in the future. Unfortunately, both of these areas have yet to reach maturity and are currently in early stages of development, so they are unlikely to contribute much margin or capital efficiency until later in the decade.

In addition to the new next-generation capability space, Lockheed's traditional platform-based businesses also represent a stable base of revenue; however, they provide very low organic growth rates in the low single-digits. This makes Lockheed's growth profile structurally inconsistent with its current valuation of ~29x earnings. Although this valuation is not overly rich historically, it does leave very little room for multiple expansion.

There is additional risk associated with the execution of the company's strategy. The integration of hypersonic technology on the Zumwalt destroyer adds some level of uncertainty related to technology and scheduling. Even with a defense supercycle, there are other macro factors that can negatively impact the upside potential of Lockheed's stock, including a shift in investor preference towards new emerging defense companies that leverage AI. After the near 30% rally, Lockheed now meets many of the characteristics of a "capital preservation" type of investment versus a source of growth. Since I believe much of the growth expectations have been priced in, I'm rating the stock a Hold.

Hypersonic Win

Lockheed’s $1.35 billion contract to provide the common peripheral system (CPS) for the U.S. Navy is a positive development. The company will be the prime contractor responsible for delivering the Navy’s first at-sea hypersonic strike capability. In fact, the USS Zumwalt will no longer have its forward gun mount; instead, it will have launch tubes installed in place of the gun mount. This makes Lockheed the primary integrator for this project. Live fire testing is expected to begin in 2026 followed by initial operational capability.

In total, the contract, when all options are included, will increase the value to just under $2 billion. Additionally, Lockheed will serve as the lead contractor for the Long-Range Hypersonic Weapon (LRHW) program for the U.S. Army.

While there is a significant amount of strategic importance associated with Lockheed being the lead contractor for this country’s first operational at-sea hypersonic platform, in terms of near-term revenue potential, this program represents a very small fraction of Lockheed's overall annual revenue. Last year, that annual revenue was $75 billion. Therefore, revenue from this new platform represents approximately 2% of Lockheed's annual revenue, which is not too impactful on additional growth or revenue opportunities for investors. Furthermore, this program is currently in the early stages of development and integration, which represents the most capital intensive, lowest margin phase of a program’s life cycle. It may take a few years before solid production and sustainment dollars flow into Lockheed from this program. With a rapid rise YTD, the market appears to have priced in the majority of expectations about this win and the overall defense supercycle.

Domestic F-35 Ceiling

The F-35 continues to serve as the primary foundation of Lockheed Martin's product line, accounting for nearly 27% of the company's total consolidated revenues in 2025. The program generates a tremendous amount of consistent, predictable revenue, which represents one of the key advantages of being such a large player in a single market. However, because there is a limit to how many F-35s can be made domestically under the allocated budget, especially when considering the Air Force and Navy have transitioned their focus to NGAD, the rate at which new F-35 airframes will be ordered by the U.S. military will no longer represent the principal source of growth that it once represented for Lockheed. Therefore, going forward, Lockheed will need to depend on both foreign sales and long-term sustainment efforts. These represent stable sources of revenue; however, they are unlikely to provide the same type of opportunity for upside potential that would support a further multiple expansion.

While Lockheed had success clearing its parked-jet inventory and produced a program-high number of aircraft in FY2025 (191), a majority of those aircraft were equipped with a partial TR-3 software package. Consequently, those aircraft are unable to perform actual combat operations. In an early 2026 report to Congress, the DOT&E reported that TR-3 software development has stalled and that no additional combat-ready versions of TR-3 have been developed since last year due to issues related to stability.

This status presents an impending financial and operational challenge. Since TR-3 is considered to be the fundamental hardware and software backbone upon which all of the Block 4 upgrades are based, the delayed delivery of TR-3 software will delay the complete upgrade capability of all F-35s until 2031, and increase the overall cost of the entire program by an estimated $6 billion. Even though the F-35 numbers look strong, investors may overlook the costs associated with retrofitting the jets for full mission readiness. This adds on more long-term execution risk, where the future software upgrades could weigh on margins even after the current defense supercycle peaks.

Rotation Risk

With a defense supercycle, institutional investors have increased allocations to the defense sector. With this supercycle, investors are looking for the next big winners; these tend to be the most attractive stocks with the highest growth potential. Therefore, as investors become more interested in high-growth defense stocks, they begin to favor AI-enabled defense technologies (e.g., Palantir's (PLTR) battlefield intelligence, autonomous systems from Anduril, etc.), there will be significant amounts of money flowing into growth stories that legacy companies (like Lockheed) might struggle to deliver.

It does not make Lockheed an outdated investment; rather, it changes what type of investment Lockheed is. This now changes the type of investor who will chase an investment like Lockheed, especially after this recent rally, where buyers will be those institutional investors who buy shares for their stable returns on equity, dividends, and defense exposure. However, institutional portfolio managers who have already seen names like Palantir increase by over 200% in less than two years and whose portfolios need to outperform the market may find it difficult to allocate additional dollars to traditional defense. With legacy defense companies like Lockheed trading at 29x trailing P/E, it may be hard to justify an investment.

This dynamic has existed for some time; however, it becomes more pronounced when Lockheed shares trade close to the upper end of their historic valuation multiples. In my view, Lockheed shares are neither sufficiently inexpensive to attract value buyers nor do they offer sufficient growth potential to attract growth buyers. This is especially true after this year's rally, where growth buyers have had strong returns. However, this growth seems now priced in, leading me to categorize it as a hold given the current story.

Execution Risk: Zumwalt's Track Record

The USS Zumwalt-class destroyers have a documented history of mission changes and cost overruns, which eventually led them to cut planned production by 91%. This kind of history should add a higher level of execution risk to this hypersonic integration program compared to other programs. The Zumwalt-class was originally designed as land-attack vessels with advanced gun systems that fired GPS-guided shells, costing $800,000 each, which was so expensive the program was quietly canceled. Now, the class is being repurposed for exactly the hypersonic strike capabilities Lockheed is supposed to deliver.

The physical integration work for the hypersonic missile tubes was completed by 2024. But live fire testing and IOC still remain to be done. Any technical delay in hypersonic integration, delay in the naval test schedule, or issue with the CPS missile itself could delay revenue recognition into future years and create margin pressure in the corresponding segment. Besides the minimal revenue, any delays or overruns, which the program has had historically, could impact margins and expected contribution to overall revenue.

Valuation

Lockheed is currently trading at approximately ~$623 with its trailing P/E multiple at ~29x. This multiple has only been growing, due to the defense supercycle and optimism on new programs. While these have merit, I believe that optimism to be priced in at current levels with the multiple at 5-year highs.

Although defense spending is at an all-time high, not all of that money will go to legacy companies like Lockheed. So far, the company has done well to extend the lifetime of its F-35 program, but future operations are still uncertain and carry significant execution risk relative to legacy projects. Lockheed will benefit, but so will up-and-comers like Palantir and Anduril, which have seen their valuations soar in recent years. At the current valuation, the rewards and risks seem fairly priced in, which leads me to rate Lockheed a Hold.

Risks

The most likely positive scenario for Lockheed would result from an earlier-than-expected TR-3 resolution. This would allow for the withholding of previously unpaid amounts by the Department of Defense and cause an acceleration in cash flows likely beyond what is currently estimated.

Global conflicts escalating to a higher level of intensity, including those with adversarial nations possessing hypersonic weapons capabilities, could lead to increased U.S. military procurement requirements and potentially advance the timeline of revenue growth.

The largest increase in funding for defense spending at the federal government level (Congress passing legislation appropriating significantly more than projected) would positively impact all defense contractors, especially a large player like Lockheed. In my view, with the stock trading at elevated multiples, I believe the majority of this is priced in.

Conclusion

Lockheed Martin continues to be one of the largest and most consistent players in the global defense market and holds a dominant position in both legacy and emerging technologies. The company's role in developing hypersonic weapons and their sustained dominance in the development of the F-35 fighter aircraft provide Lockheed with a significant place in future U.S. defense expenditures for many years to come.

However, when evaluating Lockheed from a long-term investment perspective, the company does not offer the most compelling investment narrative. As Lockheed develops its new generation of products and services, it is estimated to take at least several years before they begin to impact Lockheed's reported earnings. Lockheed is now trading at a premium multiple relative to its history, which prices in tailwinds from a defense supercycle. In addition, Lockheed's relatively low organic growth rate combined with increased competition for capital within the defense industry limits the likelihood of continued multiple expansion.

Therefore, given Lockheed's elevated valuation and current situation, the stock should be evaluated as a stable and defensive investment option rather than a high conviction buy. In my view, LMT stock is at a fair valuation where I see limited near-term upside, which is why I'm rating Lockheed a Hold.

data.title: Zai Lab: Xacduro And ZL-1310 Drive Growth Narrative
data.description:
Zai Lab Limited (ZLAB) is one of my favorites in China's pharmaceutical industry. It has a rich pipeline of product candidates focused on the treatment of cancer and immune-mediated inflammatory diseases.

But the vast majority of its revenue currently comes from agreements with Big Pharma players to commercialize their medications, such as argenx's Vyvgart (ARGX), GSK's Zejula (GSK), and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Cobenfy/Augtyro, in Greater China.

In this article, I will present 3 factors explaining why I'm initiating coverage of it with a Buy rating.

Current valuation of Zai Lab

Its revenue rose 17% year-over-year to $127.6 million in Q4. It also beat the Wall Street consensus estimate by $4.9 million.

I believe this was primarily due to a jump in net revenue of Zejula to about $56 million despite tightening competition in the recurrent ovarian cancer market from AstraZeneca's Lynparza (AZN) and BeOne's pamiparib (ONC).

Since I've discussed this PARP inhibitor in more detail previously in articles about GSK, such as "GSK: Depemokimab and Jemperli Lead Next Wave," I'll now move on to an equally important drug in Zai Lab's portfolio, Xacduro [sulbactam/durlobactam].

But first, I will remind you that Xacduro was approved by China's NMPA on May 20, 2024, for the treatment of pneumonia caused by bacteria called Acinetobacter.

Sadly, Acinetobacter is difficult to treat due to its high levels of antibiotic resistance. And so before the launch of sulbactam/durlobactam, there was an urgent medical need for new antibiotics.

But why do I think Xacduro can be called a "savior"?

In the Phase 3 ATTACK study [NCT03894046], it reduced 28-day all-cause mortality by 13.2% compared to the "gold standard," colistin [P<0.001].

And this clinical data is reflecting positively on the sales of Xacduro.

So, it generated about $10.7 million in revenue for Zai Lab in Q4, up 66.5% QoQ and beating my "base case" scenario by $1.5 million.

Zai Lab's pipeline of experimental drugs

Overall, it has a rich pipeline of molecules at various stages of clinical development.

But among these, I believe ZL-1310 [zocilurtatug pelitecan] has the greatest commercial potential.

Zocilurtatug pelitecan is an antibody-drug conjugate.

That is, one part of its molecule consists of an anti-DLL3 monoclonal antibody, which is connected via a tripeptide-based linker to an anticancer agent, a topoisomerase 1 inhibitor (marked in red for clarity).

This experimental drug interested me for 3 reasons.

It is being developed against a very common and aggressive type of cancer called SCLC [small cell lung cancer], which accounts for about 15% of all lung cancer diagnoses.

To my great regret, WHO's Cancer Tomorrow doesn't provide numbers only for extensive-stage SCLC, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is big, as about 4.62 million cases of trachea, bronchus and lung [2.95 million men and 1.67 million women] will be diagnosed in 2050, up 86.2% rise from 2022.

Secondly, on April 1st, Zai Lab announced a partnership with Amgen (AMGN). Under the deal, Amgen will fund the Phase 1b trial to evaluate the efficacy of ZL-1310 with its DLL3/CD3 bispecific antibody, Imdelltra.

In my view, since these drugs have complementary mechanisms, this could potentially help overcome resistance pathways, increasing OS and progression-free survival.

And the last reason, of course, is the results of the Phase 1study [NCT06179069].

In it, pre-treated patients with ES-SCLC who received 1.6 mg/kg ZL-1310 as 2L therapy achieved the best ORR [overall response rate] of 68%, which is higher than blockbusters such as AstraZeneca's Imfinzi (AZN) or Bristol-Myers Squibb's Opdivo.

Long-Term prospects of Zai Lab

I expect its revenue to continue to grow until 2030, mainly due to increased sales of Nuzyra, Xacduro, and Zejula.

By the way, net revenue of the "gem" called Nuzyra for the treatment of ABSSSI and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia was $16 million in Q4, up 45.5% YoY and 3.7% quarter-on-quarter.

Also, in my DCF model, I factored potential approvals of its product candidates, including povetacicept [IgA nephropathy], zocilurtatug pelitecan [ES-SCLC], and elegrobart [active thyroid eye disease] by the NMPA.

And in forecasting Zai Lab's revenue and EBIT, I took into account the subdued demand for Vyvgart/Vyvgart Hytrulo, as well as tightening competition in the non-small cell lung cancer market, especially from Eisai/Nuvation's taletrectinib (ESALF) (NUVB).

Thus, in my estimation, its total revenue will be $1.56 billion in 2030, which firstly implies its annual growth from 9.7% to 47.9%, and secondly, it is in line with the expectations of 4 analysts [$1.22 billion to $1.7 billion].

Accordingly, this will lead to a drop in Zai Lab's price-to-sales ratio from 4.86x over the last 12 months to below 1.5x.

What are my and Wall Street analysts' expectations for Zai Lab's earnings over the next 5 years?

And here, things are worse relative to its revenue. So, the consensus estimate for Q1 is -0.48 cents [from -0.58 cents to -0.41 cents], representing a year-over-year drop of 6.8%.

However, in my view, not everything is so bad if I look at the broader picture.

So, given Zai Lab's revenue growth rate I mentioned earlier and my assumption that Tivdak will be approved by the NMPA in mid-May 2026 for the treatment of cervical cancer, this will lead to triple/double-digit growth in its non-GAAP EPS [from 36.3% to 660%] between 2027 and 2030.

Also, according to my model, this financial metric is above the median of the Wall Street analysts' forecast range [$0.4-$2.1]. Because I have more optimistic expectations regarding the sales of "duo," Nuzyra and Xacduro.

As a result, ZLAB's earnings per share will be $1.52 in 2030, which corresponds to a price-to-earnings ratio of 13.4x, 22.1% lower than the sector median.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with risks.

They include, among others, potential competition from Merck's Keytruda (MRK) and Jazz Pharmaceuticals' Zepzelca (JAZZ), and RFK Jr./Trump's information pressure on the sector.

Takeaway

2026 will be a year of multiple key data readouts and catalysts for Zai Lab.

So, I'm especially waiting for the launch of Cobenfy in China in Q2 for the treatment of schizophrenia, which, by the way, affects about 5.3 million people.

And in addition to the investment theses I highlighted earlier in the article, another one is that the average price target for Zai Lab is $38.47.

That is, this implies an upside of about 88.5% from the current ZLAB stock price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Stock Market Rally: Buy Or Fade It?
data.description:
Last week, the stock market rally was one of the best performances in nearly a year. The S&P 500 surged 3.4%, the Nasdaq climbed 4.4%, and the bulls declared the correction over. As I have stated before, having watched markets for more than 35 years, I have come to recognize the difference between a relief rally and the end of a corrective cycle. So far, this remains a relief rally until overhead resistance is broken through and successfully retested. The question that matters now is whether the stock market rally has the institutional support to break through those resistance levels or whether Monday’s open will reveal the reversal was already finished before most investors realized it started.

The answer, based on every technical and macro lens I use, points heavily toward the latter.

Let’s Review The Tape

The S&P 500 closed at 6,582, bouncing roughly 4.5% off the late-March lows near 6,300. That sounds constructive until you examine what those price levels represent. The index pushed directly into its 200-day moving average near 6,642 and remains comfortably below the 50-day moving average near 6,789. The 20-DMA trends around 6,607. Together, those three levels form an overhead cluster of resistance that has historically acted as a gravitational ceiling for stocks in corrective environments. Furthermore, the volume profile we analyzed on Wednesday confirmed the same. Many investors are trapped at those prices, holding positions at a loss and waiting for any rally to exit at break-even, which adds to the risk of a relief rally failure.

Then came Thursday’s escalation, following President Trump’s prime-time address, which offered no clear path toward ending the conflict or reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Unsurprisingly, oil responded immediately. WTI crude surged to $111.54 per barrel on settlement Thursday, the highest close since June 2022. Equity futures reversed what the bulls had built over two sessions, trading sharply lower Thursday morning before recovering to a small gain by the close. The only thing that likely helped the market end on a good note was that markets were closed for Good Friday. With the jobs report posting a gain of 178,000 on Friday, the market would likely have sold off on the expectation that the Federal Reserve is now fully trapped in terms of monetary policy.

With that said, we need to focus on what the market is telling us, which is where the MFBR index comes in.

Why You Should Listen To The MFBR Index

Our Money Flow Breadth Ratio, a rules-based institutional flow indicator we’ve backtested across 1,351 weekly observations from 2000 through 2025, currently stands at 35% and is declining.

That places it squarely in the single worst sub-range within SELL territory and historically suggests a higher degree of caution. The MFBR works by tracking weekly net dollar money flow in the S&P 500 index, counting the number of the last 20 weeks that showed positive institutional buying, and converting that count into a ratio. Readings above 60% signal sustained buying pressure, whereas readings below 40% signal sustained selling pressure. Notably, the model goes a step further and also incorporates the direction of the MFBR, whether it is rising or falling. This is because a 25-year backtest from 2000 to 2025 demonstrated that trajectory matters as much as level, particularly in transition zones.

At 35% and declining, the data is unambiguous about what history suggests will happen next. Across 73 observations in the 35–40% zone, average forward returns were negative at one month (−1.1%), three months (−1.2%), and six months (−0.3%). The six-month win rate was just 34.7%, meaning the market was lower six months later nearly two out of every three times. The twelve-month win rate recovered to 58.6%, but that is still well below the all-period baseline of 75.7%. The backtest’s range of outcomes, from a best-case of +66.6% to a worst-case of −29.1% over 12 months, underscores why maximum defensiveness is the appropriate posture right now.

Bottom line: Until the MFBR stabilizes and recovers sustainably above 50%, patience over aggression and capital preservation over return-chasing are the rules. The one exception: if the MFBR drops below 30%, historical data shows a genuine contrarian buy signal with a 100% win rate at twelve months. We are not there yet.

That is just the technical data; other factors are at play that may also have an impact.

Other Risks: Oil, Interest Rates & The Fed’s Dilemma

The macro backdrop has rarely been this unfavorable for equities simultaneously across so many dimensions. Oil is the primary driver right now, with Brent crude oil spot price rising sharply following the onset of military action in the Middle East. With petroleum shipments through the Strait of Hormuz being disrupted and significant Middle East production shut in, recession risks are rising. The longer the situation persists, the greater the risk that the oil impulse translates into broader inflationary pressures. (As shown, there is a decent correlation between oil prices and CPI.)

That puts the Federal Reserve in a genuinely impossible position. It is also one that should concern every equity investor far more than the day-to-day volatility. Markets are now pricing zero rate cuts for the remainder of 2026. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) closed at 4.31%, while the 30-year yield (US30Y) was 4.88%. Fed Chair Powell has explicitly acknowledged the tension.

“Inflation remains above target, the labor market is softening, and the conflict has introduced significant uncertainty into every forecasting model the FOMC relies on.”

This is the risk where energy-driven inflation squeezes real purchasing power while employment deteriorates. The Fed cannot cut without inflaming inflation expectations, and it cannot raise rates without breaking an already-fragile labor market. The result is paralysis at exactly the moment markets need a clear policy anchor.

The jobs report adds another layer of complexity. March’s nonfarm payrolls print came in at +178,000. That was much stronger than the 60,000 estimates, and the unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%. With the market closed on Friday, the reaction will be delayed until Monday’s opening.

Furthermore, last week’s stock market rally was not broad, which matters far more than the headline index gain suggests. As of April 2, just 27.6% of S&P 500 constituents were trading above their 50-day moving averages. That is up from below 20% at the March low but down ~70% during the broad-based advance earlier this year. The percentage of S&P 500 stocks above their 200-day moving average sits below 50%. These types of readings are more akin to corrections and bear markets than bull markets.

There are positives to consider. Relative strength (RSI) on the S&P 500 recovered from deeply oversold levels near 30 in late March to approximately 45. That is encouraging from a mean-reversion perspective; however, it is still below the neutral 50 level. The VIX is holding near 28, confirming that institutional hedging demand remains elevated. When professionals are still buying protection at these levels, they are telling you something about their conviction in the rally. Momentum, as measured by the MACD indicator, remains in negative territory but did trigger a short-term buy signal. However, these momentum conditions are consistent with what we’ve seen historically in corrective environments. Short-covering bounces tend to fade back into the primary downtrend.

April seasonality deserves acknowledgment. Historically, April has been one of the stronger months for equities. But that historical average includes far calmer geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrops than today’s. A seasonal tailwind is a weak force compared to oil at $111, a frozen Fed, and a still weak labor market that hasn’t fully felt the oil price spike. Furthermore, the MFBR data also points to sustained institutional selling pressure.

So, while there are certainly reasons to be bullish, there is risk in that view.

Where The Bullish Case Potentially Fails

I take the bull case seriously because good analysis requires it, and there are genuine counterarguments worth acknowledging.

  • First, the MFBR’s own 25-year backtest shows that when the ratio drops below 30%, a true capitulation washout has occurred. The subsequent returns flip dramatically positive: +5.1% at one month, +7.8% at six months, and a 100% win rate at twelve months. We’re not there yet, but that threshold isn’t far from the current 35% reading.
  • Second, the volume profile below 6,300 thins out considerably. The lack of sellers could allow prices to squeeze back toward the resistance without much friction if buyers step in.
  • Third, the first-quarter earnings season begins next week. FactSet currently projects roughly 13% S&P 500 earnings growth. If that number is confirmed by actual results, it could provide the fundamental catalyst bulls need.

The problem is that none of those factors changes the immediate risk-reward math. The 200-DMA remains resistant on a closing basis, despite last week’s stock market rally. Breadth has not recovered. Institutional money flow is in sell territory and falling. Oil is near a multi-year high with no clear resolution in sight. And the biggest monthly economic data release of April dropped on a closed market, deferring the full reaction to Monday. At the current MFBR reading, the historical six-month win rate is 34.7%. That reading indicates that selling near this resistance remains the correct trade in roughly 2 out of 3 comparable setups.

For now, the burden of proof is on the bulls, and last week’s rally has not yet met it.

As such, following last week’s stock market rally, here are seven actions to consider when trading begins on Monday.

The summer sets up as one of the more difficult stretches for equities in recent memory. Oil above $100 for any sustained period has historically pressured both consumer spending and corporate margins simultaneously. The Fed will unlikely be able to provide a rate-cutting tailwind that markets previously relied on. Finally, the labor market, which was already softening before this conflict began, is now doing so with the added headwind of energy-driven inflation. None of that means a market crash is inevitable. However, it does mean that the risk-reward profile of being aggressively long equities at current levels is poor and that patience, at least for now, is its own form of return.

Defense over offense. Trade accordingly.

Original Post
